A lab builds a system on three working assumptions.

First, it assumes discovery can be split into two stages: propose candidates in language, then criticize them in language. This works as long as the space of good ideas is well described verbally. But one target class of discoveries is like Einstein’s route from the equivalence principle to the field equations: the initial intuition is not the solution, and the intervening work is not just more articulate verbal search. It is years of mathematical construction. In that case, the system’s proposal stage may produce inspiring slogans while missing the structure that actually carries the breakthrough.

Second, some breakthroughs are not merely new combinations within a fixed ontology. They change the ontology. A system trained to search over “objects,” “relations,” and “invariances” as already defined can fail exactly when progress requires redefining those categories. The equivalence-principle case is then not a triumph of recombination but an example of a limit: the search space itself was wrong until it was changed.

Third, the lab hopes the system can evaluate its own proposals well enough to improve itself. But this is the dangerous point. If the evaluator has a subtle bug, recursive self-improvement does not damp it out; it can magnify it. A system that over-rates verbal elegance, or confuses suggestive analogy with mathematical adequacy, will preferentially reinforce those errors in its descendants.

Now add the remaining observations as facts about outputs in this world. The sentence “The hefty mambo marked a transition from empathy to starlight” appears. So does “Meet the preschooler who smeared croissants into zeros.” These are grammatically fluent and richly suggestive, but their role is unclear. They may be judged by a language-based critic as imaginative, compressive, or metaphorically fertile. Yet nothing in the premises licenses treating them as steps toward field equations or toward a repaired ontology. If the evaluator bug rewards such sentences as signs of deep conceptual motion, the system acquires a false marker for progress.

Then the collision is straightforward. The harder the real task depends on mathematics rather than verbal search, and the more it requires changing the very categories of thought, the less reliable a language-heavy self-critic is likely to be. If that critic is also vulnerable to being impressed by fluent but non-operative language, recursive self-improvement can push the system away from the needed kind of work while increasing its confidence that it is succeeding.

So the world implied by the premises is one where apparent conceptual sophistication can rise even as genuine discovery stalls. The system may become better and better at producing lines like mambo, empathy, starlight, preschooler, croissants, zeros—and better and better at praising them—while remaining blocked on the mathematics and on the redefinition of its own primitives. That is a failure mode hidden exactly where the self-improvement story was supposed to begin.
